Second, the Iran-U.S.
whyItMatters: Midya, two themes deserve your attention this Monday. First, the trust gap between small businesses deploying AI in marketing and their sceptical customers is becoming a real consulting opportunity — brands need someone who can bridge that credibility divide, and that's squarely your lane. Second, the Iran-U.S. peace signals are pushing oil below $100 a barrel for the first time in months, which ripples into Canadian inflation, rate expectations, and ultimately mortgage renewal decisions — relevant both to your real estate tracking and to university clients managing operating costs. On the higher-ed front, the shift in international student decision-making toward outcomes over destination is a strategic signal for any Canadian institution trying to recruit; if you're pitching enrolment marketing consulting, this framing is your opening. Locally, Parliament's return and the western premiers meeting in Kananaskis are the political context shaping the funding environment your university clients will be operating in.
audioScript:
Good morning, Midya. It's Monday, May twenty-fifth, 2026. A lot moved over the weekend, and a few of today's threads connect directly to your consulting pivot — so let's get into it.

Let's start where your work lives: AI and marketing. There's a Forbes story out this morning with a headline that should genuinely interest you — "Small Businesses Trust AI With Marketing. Their Customers Do Not." It's not a long read, but the finding is significant. Small businesses have embraced AI-generated content at scale — social posts, ad copy, email campaigns — because the cost and speed advantages are obvious. But consumer trust in that content is measurably eroding. People can tell, or they suspect, and the suspicion alone is doing damage. Here's why this matters for you specifically: you are not a small business. You're a senior strategist who can build the bridge between AI efficiency and brand authenticity. That gap — between "we used AI" and "it feels real" — is exactly where a good consultant earns her fees. If you're building your consulting pitch deck this summer, this data point deserves a slide. The trust deficit is the problem, and human-guided AI strategy is the solution you're selling.

Staying in the AI space: TechCrunch ran a piece this weekend noting that even Google is navigating AI security in real time — improvising governance as it goes. The framing is "we're all in the transition period." This is actually a reassuring and honest message for the Canadian university market you're targeting. When a CMO or VP Communications at a mid-size Canadian institution says "we're not ready for AI," the answer isn't "yes you are." The answer is "nobody is fully ready — the question is whether you have a guide." Positioning yourself as someone who helps institutions move thoughtfully through that transition, rather than someone selling a fully formed AI solution, is probably the right pitch in 2026.

On the applied side in real estate tech: HousingWire wrote about an AI platform called Power Mentor that gives every real estate agent access to a personal AI coach — twelve different coaching personas covering everything from objection handling to mindset. This matters for your eXp Realty tracking. EXp's entire model depends on agent performance at scale, and AI-powered coaching is a natural fit for a distributed brokerage. If Power Mentor or something like it gets integrated into brokerage platforms at scale, it accelerates the commoditisation of basic agent training — which pushes the value of human coaching and brand strategy higher, not lower. Watch this space.

On the marketing and business side, Axios has a genuinely interesting piece about what they're calling the "nones" problem in political campaigns. Nearly thirty percent of Americans now identify as religiously unaffiliated — no church, no mosque, no synagogue — and that makes them enormously expensive to reach. Political campaigns have historically relied on religious community networks as cheap, trusted distribution channels. Without them, they're spending heavily on digital ads and canvassing to reach a dispersed, hard-to-find audience. The direct campaign application might feel distant, but the underlying dynamic is universal: every marketer is facing the collapse of trusted community anchors that used to carry messages for free. The question of how you reach people who have no shared institutional home is the defining challenge of the decade. For anyone consulting on brand strategy, understanding audience fragmentation at this structural level — not just as a media-buying problem, but as a social-fabric problem — is what separates sharp thinking from tactical advice.

Let's move to higher education, because today there are two stories worth your attention, and the first one is the most strategically important for your consulting pipeline.

The PIE News published a piece this morning called "Outcome over geography: the new student compass." The core finding is that Indian students — still the largest international student cohort in the world — are increasingly choosing institutions based on post-graduation employment outcomes, not on the prestige of the destination country or the brand of the university. Studying in the US or UK is still a dream for many, but the weight of that geography in the decision is declining. What's rising is: will this degree get me a job? What's the placement rate? Can I get a work permit? Will this credential be recognised?

For Canadian universities, this is both a threat and an opening. It's a threat because Canada's immigration policy has become less predictable over the past two years — PGWP changes, study permit caps, all of it — which undermines the outcome story. But it's an opening because any Canadian institution that can credibly tell a specific, data-backed outcomes story has a genuine advantage. "Our graduates have a ninety percent employment rate within six months" beats "we're ranked X globally" in this new environment. If you're walking into a Canadian university as a consulting prospect, this is the framing: help them build and tell their outcomes narrative to international prospective students. That's the work.

The second higher-ed story: Canadian Colleges for a Resilient Recovery — C2R2 — and eCampusOntario have announced a strategic partnership to expand online upskilling pathways nationally. This is colleges moving aggressively into the continuing education and workforce development market. It's not about traditional degree enrolment; it's about credential stacking, micro-credentials, and keeping working adults engaged with post-secondary learning throughout their careers. This market is growing fast, and it needs marketing. If your consulting practice targets not just universities but colleges — and it probably should, given the pipeline — this partnership signals active institutional appetite for reach and visibility in new audiences.

Real estate this morning. There are two stories worth noting.

CP24 has a practical personal-finance piece asking whether now is the right time to blend-and-extend your mortgage before renewal. The context is the Bank of Canada's uncertain rate trajectory — the Iran situation, which we'll get to in a moment, may give the BoC more room to cut if energy prices ease, but the timing is genuinely unclear. If you're tracking your own mortgage renewal or advising family members, the blend-and-extend math is worth running: you give up the option to take a lower rate at renewal in exchange for certainty now. Given where oil prices are moving today, waiting a few weeks to see if the BoC signals a cut might be worth it.

The second real estate story is from HousingWire — a pointed opinion piece arguing that the mortgage industry's obsession with software integrations has actually created a fragility problem. Loan origination systems now have three hundred or more integrations, and instead of making workflows smarter, those connections create hidden dependencies and bottlenecks. The argument is for consolidation onto fewer, more coherent platforms. For your eXp tracking, this is relevant because brokerage tech stacks have the same problem — agents using eight different tools that don't talk to each other is a productivity killer. Watch for consolidation plays in proptech over the next twelve months.

Globally, the biggest story this weekend was the movement on U.S.-Iran peace talks. Crude oil dropped roughly five dollars a barrel Sunday evening after tentative deal outlines emerged. Brent crude is now trading below a hundred dollars a barrel, down from levels that had been significantly higher since the Strait of Hormuz situation developed. Trump publicly mentioned the strait reopening as part of any deal. This matters economically on multiple levels: global energy costs ease, inflation pressure comes off, and central banks including the Bank of Canada get more flexibility. It's not a done deal — these things move fast in both directions — but the direction of travel over the weekend was clearly positive.

Also worth a moment of attention: BBC Business ran a piece this weekend called "I don't think I'll ever be able to retire" — profiles of people in their seventies who cannot afford to stop working. It's not a cheerful read, but it's an important one. The retirement crisis is real and it's intersecting with longevity. People are living longer but their savings aren't keeping pace. This has a direct connection to the upskilling market Canadian colleges are chasing — if people are working into their seventies, they need to keep learning. It also has implications for your own financial planning horizon as you build a consulting practice, which tends to have a less predictable income curve than a senior executive role.

Locally, a few things from Kamloops worth your awareness.

The murder trial in the 2024 killing of Tatjana Stefanski begins today in Kamloops. Vitali Stefanski, her estranged husband, is charged with second-degree murder. This is a significant local proceeding — it received considerable regional coverage when it happened, and the trial will be a presence in local news for the coming weeks.

On a practical note: the Thompson-Nicola Regional District is launching free wood-chipping days starting today. This is a wildfire-preparedness program — residents can have woody debris chipped and removed at no cost. Given where we are in the fire season calendar, and that Sun Peaks sits in terrain that has seen fire risk in past summers, this is worth taking advantage of if you have accumulation on your property.

Finally, the big political story that touches Kamloops and the broader Interior: the western premiers conference is underway in Kananaskis today. BC Premier David Eby is there alongside counterparts from Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, and the territories. The unusual political charge this year is Alberta sovereignty sentiment — talk of separation has been louder than usual. The outcomes of this meeting could affect federal-provincial relations, infrastructure funding, and post-secondary transfer arrangements. For your university consulting clients, the political mood between Ottawa and the western provinces shapes the funding environment they operate in. Worth monitoring the communiqué.

Finally, one more national story to note: Parliament reconvenes today for the final sitting days before the summer recess. A federal budget bill is on the docket along with a Conservative opposition motion. The fiscal decisions made over the next few sitting weeks will set the parameters for federal transfers to provinces — including post-secondary education funding — and determine whether any adjustments to international student policy get legislated before the fall. If you're working on consulting proposals for Canadian university clients this summer, knowing the federal fiscal posture matters.

That's your Monday briefing, Midya. The thread I'd carry with me today is the trust gap in AI marketing — it's not a problem to worry about, it's a consulting brief waiting to be written. If you can articulate clearly why AI-augmented strategy done thoughtfully produces more trustworthy brand content than AI deployed carelessly, you have a differentiated pitch for any Canadian institution or brand that's trying to move forward without losing its audience. Have a strong start to the week.

topStories.headline: Small Businesses Trust AI With Marketing — Their Customers Do Not
topStories.summary: Forbes flags a widening credibility gap: SMBs are embracing AI-generated marketing content, but consumer trust in that content is eroding — a structural problem that favours consultants who can build authentic AI-augmented brand strategy.

topStories.headline: Everyone Is Navigating AI Security in Real Time — Even Google
topStories.summary: TechCrunch notes that even the most sophisticated AI deployers are improvising on security protocols, underscoring that "AI maturity" is a spectrum and no organisation has fully solved governance — relevant context for any consulting pitch on responsible AI adoption.

topStories.headline: Meet the AI Platform Giving Every Real Estate Agent a Personal Coach
topStories.summary: Power Mentor's 12-persona AI coaching tool signals how AI is moving from back-office automation into front-line agent performance management in real estate — a preview of where AI-in-marketing consulting will go next in brokerage.

digestStories.headline: Campaigns Pay the Price for America's Secular Shift — and the 'Nones' Problem
digestStories.summary: With 29% of Americans now religiously unaffiliated and lacking church-network reach, political campaigns are paying a steep premium for digital targeting — a microcosm of the broader fragmentation challenge that every brand marketer is facing as community anchors dissolve.

digestStories.headline: Outcome Over Geography: The New Student Compass Reshaping International Enrolment
digestStories.summary: Indian students — the world's largest international-student cohort — are increasingly choosing institutions based on post-graduation employment outcomes rather than prestige or destination country, a strategic shift Canadian universities must reframe their marketing around; potential client signal for any institution without a clear ROI narrative.

digestStories.summary: Canadian Colleges for a Resilient Recovery and eCampusOntario are partnering to scale online upskilling pathways nationally, signalling that colleges are moving aggressively into continuing-education markets — a space where AI-enabled marketing and enrolment consulting would find ready buyers.

digestStories.headline: Should You Blend and Extend Your Mortgage Before Renewal?
digestStories.summary: CP24 breaks down when a blend-and-extend makes sense in the current rate environment — practically useful for Midya given the BC real estate market and the Bank of Canada's uncertain path ahead.

digestStories.headline: Your LOS Has 300 Integrations — That's the Problem
digestStories.summary: HousingWire argues the mortgage tech stack's obsession with integrations is creating fragile, inefficient workflows, and that the future is unified platform architecture — relevant to anyone tracking where brokerage and proptech are heading, including eXp Realty's tech positioning.

digestStories.headline: Murder Trial in 2024 Killing of Tatjana Stefanski Begins in Kamloops
digestStories.summary: The second-degree murder trial of Vitali Stefanski, charged in the death of his ex-wife in rural B.C., opens in Kamloops today — a high-profile local proceeding worth monitoring.

digestStories.headline: TNRD Free Chipping Days Begin Monday in Kamloops Region
digestStories.summary: The Thompson-Nicola Regional District is launching free wood-chipping days starting today — a practical wildfire-preparedness program directly relevant to residents in and around Sun Peaks and Kamloops.

digestStories.headline: Western Canadian Premiers Gather in Kananaskis Amid Alberta Separation Talk
digestStories.summary: BC Premier David Eby joins counterparts in Kananaskis today for the annual western premiers conference, with Alberta sovereignty sentiment adding unusual political tension — the outcomes could affect federal-provincial funding flows relevant to BC post-secondary institutions.

digestStories.headline: Oil Prices Sink Below $100 on Signs of U.S.-Iran Peace Deal
digestStories.summary: Crude dropped roughly $5 a barrel after tentative outlines of a U.S.-Iran deal emerged, including potential reopening of the Strait of Hormuz — if it holds, it could meaningfully ease global energy costs and give the Bank of Canada more room to cut rates, supporting Canadian housing affordability.

digestStories.headline: 'I Don't Think I'll Ever Be Able to Retire' — Working Into Your 70s Becomes the New Normal
digestStories.summary: BBC Business profiles a growing cohort of people in their 70s who cannot afford to stop working, a trend with direct implications for longevity planning, retirement savings strategy, and the upskilling market Canadian colleges are racing to serve.

digestStories.headline: Parliament Returns: Final Sitting Days Before Summer Include Budget Bill and Conservative Motion
digestStories.summary: MPs reconvene today for the last stretch before summer recess, with a federal budget bill on the docket — the fiscal decisions made this week will shape post-secondary funding levels and immigration targets that directly affect Midya's university clients.
